content.problemStatement:
You are given an integer nnn, and your task is to generate all binary strings of length nnn that **do not contain consecutive `1`s**.

The strings must be printed in **lexicographically increasing order** (sorted order).

A binary string is a string that contains only the characters `'0'` and `'1'`.

Function DeclarationFunction Namegenerategenerategenerate – This function generates all binary strings of length **`n`** that do not contain consecutive `1`s using **recursion**, and prints them in lexicographically increasing order.

Parameters
nnn : A number representing the required length of the binary strings.
Return Value
The function **does not return anything**.
It prints all valid binary strings of length nnn, each on a new line.
Constraints
1≤n≤201 \le n \le 201≤n≤20
The string contains only characters 000 and 111
No binary string may contain 111111 as a substring

Input Format

The first line contains a single integer nnn — the length of the binary string.


Output Format

Print all valid binary strings of length nnn that do **not contain consecutive 111s**.
Each string must be printed on a **new line**.
Strings must appear in **lexicographically increasing order**.
constraints: The total number of printed strings fits easily in memory for the given limits.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T (1 ≤ T ≤ 10) – the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer n (1 ≤ n ≤ 20) – the required length of the binary strings.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print all binary strings of length n that do not contain two consecutive ‘1’s. The strings of a single test case must be printed in lexicographically increasing order, each on its own line. The outputs of different test cases are concatenated one after another (no extra blank lines).

sampleTestCases.explanation: All binary strings of length 4 without two consecutive ‘1’s are listed in lexicographic order.
All binary strings of length 3 without two consecutive ‘1’s are listed in lexicographic order.
All binary strings of length 2 without two consecutive ‘1’s are listed in lexicographic order.

solution.approach: We generate the strings recursively from left to right. At each position we may always place a ‘0’. We may place a ‘1’ only if the previous character is not ‘1’ (or we are at the first position). The recursion stops when the current length equals n, at which point the built string is printed. Because the recursion first tries ‘0’ and then ‘1’, the produced strings are automatically in lexicographic order. The algorithm is executed independently for each test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(F_{n+2}) per test case, where F is the Fibonacci sequence, because the number of valid strings of length n equals F_{n+2}. Each string is printed once, so the total work is proportional to the output size.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(n) – the recursion depth and the temporary buffer/store for the current prefix.
